chapters.title: Control your thoughts, control your life
chapters.text:
We know that system one goes by what it has experienced, therefore creating memories, and system two works on logic, ruling our conscious thinking. The problem comes when trying to make a decision about something that we disliked in the past or felt emotional about. This situation causes a 'tug-of-war' effect between the two systems.

Our memory prioritizes peak emotional moments over duration or overall experience. Medical patients rating painful procedures remember almost nothing about how long they lasted — only the worst moment and how it ended. This is why colonoscopies with an extra minute of mild discomfort (but better ending) are rated more positively than shorter, objectively less painful ones.

What does this do? It may cause you to avoid that place or situation in the future so that you don't go through that experience again. It's a form of protection, in some ways. Yet, logic says that we're unlikely to experience the same thing again in the same place. Putting the brakes on an emotional decision and exploring all angles is difficult, but awareness is critical. Otherwise, we run the risk of missing out on great adventures and opportunities.

Of course, some decisions in life involve the risk of loss. Neither system likes that at all, so they will always try to push us toward the safest bet regardless of the probable outcome. For instance, if you know the ex-love of your life is likely to be at a party you've been invited to, you'll want to avoid it because you know it's going to hurt. The same goes for gambling. When the probability of a loss is higher than that of a win, you may instinctively want to avoid it. Of course, many people go against their instincts, but the pull is still there.

Loss aversion explains why people won't take a 50/50 bet to win $200 or lose $100, even though it's mathematically profitable. The fear of losing $100 feels twice as strong as the excitement of gaining $200.

According to research by Kahneman and Tversky, these unconscious biases have measurable impacts: they influence salary negotiations (creating an average 18% difference in outcomes), investment returns (costing individual investors 2-3% annually), and even judicial sentencing (with variations up to 20% based on irrelevant factors like time of day).

Rewards and punishments mold your reactions and motivate your actions — pay attention to them.

chapters.title: Are you system one or system two?
chapters.text:
Modern science is still far from completely understanding the human brain. The complex gray lump of matter in our skull has captivated the attention of scientists since the dawn of time. It's pretty amazing when you think about it. However, psychologists Keith Stanovich and Richard West believe that we all have two systems for thinking:

• System one: Operates automatically and quickly without voluntary control and with little effort. Responsible for fast thinking.
• System two: Allocates attention to heavy mental activity of any kind. Responsible for slow thinking.

Here's a quick test: What's 2+2? That instant answer was system one. Now calculate 17x24 in your head. Feel your brain shift gears? That's system two reluctantly taking over. System one handles about 95% of your daily decisions, but the wrong 5% can derail everything.

While both systems are unique, they often work together. In fact, system two has a habit of micromanaging what system one is doing. We know that the human brain usually looks for the easy way out, but it's essential to learn about these two systems to understand how your brain thinks.

Let's break it down.

System one is a habitual decision-maker, influenced by choices you made in the past and your intuition. It often jumps to conclusions because it's not concerned with accuracy and wants to save time and energy. System two is like a skeptical detective who questions everything, but it's lazy and expensive to run, so it only shows up when system one admits it's confused. When you're stuck with a difficult decision, system two activates to delve into the problem and find the best solution.

When system one is behaving itself, it asks for help from its more "sensible" sibling (system two) if it can't find a way out of a tricky situation. This is when the two systems work together to find a way around the problem. However, the problem comes when system one decides to go rogue and doesn't ask for help from system two. It assumes it can handle the issue on its own to save energy, and that's when jumping to conclusions happens.

Your logical thinking system has limited battery life and goes offline exactly when you need it most.

The more we are exposed to information and create ideas around what we learn, the more biases we develop. That’s why it's so important to check the information we read online to ensure we believe the truth and not an altered version. Why is this important? Because system one loves to believe what it's told.

chapters.title: Do you believe the facts?
chapters.text:
Cognitive bias affects so much of our lives that we don't even realize it.

For example, when you see a product labeled "Customer's #1 Choice," your brain accepts this without asking. Yet, out of how many options? Which customers? When was this measured? According to research by Shu and Carlson published in the Journal of Consumer Research (2014), people rate products significantly higher when they carry these vague superiority claims, even when the claims are meaningless.

The anchoring effect occurs when a particular value for an unknown quantity influences your estimate of the whole.

To assess this phenomenon, Amos Tversky and Daniel Kahneman created a fortune wheel rigged to stop at 10 or 65. Students of the University of Oregon participated in this experiment. They were supposed to spin the wheel and then answer two questions about the percentage of African nations in the UN.

The first one was: "Is that percentage higher or lower than the wheel's number?" The second one was: "What’s your guess about the actual percentage?" Those who drew 10 from the wheel gave lower percentages, and those with 65 answered with higher ones. Therefore, the random number they noticed before answering the questions influenced their judgment subconsciously.

All of this is an example of how our brains try desperately to save energy. You can understand why: it's trying to ensure the rest of the bodily organs are working correctly. You could say it's got more important things on its plate. However, if we're unaware of these biases, we could end up making incorrect choices and assumptions. So, while your brain isn't trying to trick you per se, it's not stopping to look at the hard facts.

The psychological mechanisms that produce anchoring make us far more suggestible than most of us would want to be. ~ Daniel Kahneman

Daniel Kahneman,

A similar effect happens with local news coverage. After seeing reports about a neighborhood break-in, people overestimate local crime rates by up to 300%, according to research published in the Journal of Communication (2003). This occurs even when official statistics show crime is actually decreasing in their area. The key is to apply rational thinking and explore all sides.

chapters.title: Statistics vs. real-life experiences
chapters.text:
System two loves statistics. If it could read books full of them, it would. That’s because we can only make strong arguments and choices by arming ourselves with the facts. However, that doesn't mean that statistics are always accurate or a good indicator of what might happen next.

Let's suppose you're from a place where humans don't eat meat. In that case, you'd struggle to believe that humans are responsible for the death of 65% of animals worldwide. That's because our everyday lives have shaped our way of thinking, and something different causes us to stop and widen our eyes. That's system one, but then system two kicks in and helps us assess the situation more carefully.

Also, regardless of existing facts and statistics, a situation is not always bound to follow a regular pattern. For example, if a serial killer has terrorized a community for a year, it doesn't mean the next murder will be committed by the same person. However, existing facts help in the absence of any other information.

Predictions are helpful planning tools, but shouldn't be relied upon as fact.

Of course, predictions and forecasts are a part of everyday life. Across all fields of study, experts and professionals make predictions based on calculations and the facts available. We all do it. We base predictions on recent occurrences, experiences, or familiarity. We deduce what might happen next by seeing specific cues.

To produce unbiased predictions, we must start with the average information available and then gradually work toward getting more data from existing guidelines, statistics, and facts. Again, we're working with system two.

You might wonder how you can stop making intuitive predictions not based on solid facts. The truth is that you can't stop it completely; it's part of your brain (system one), but you can control it by training yourself to identify these predictions when they occur. Don't just accept everything as fact; question it and dig in a little. Let system two do its work.

If you care about being thought credible and intelligent, do not use complex language where simpler language will do. ~ Daniel Kahneman

Daniel Kahneman,

After all, illusions shape the way we view and approach life. That’s why misinformation is so dangerous. When we view information rewritten to fit a certain agenda, we shape our biases according to it. Exploring different viewpoints and challenging anything that seems shocking or too good to be true is far more reliable.

Did you know? According to Media Statistics, 52% of Americans regularly encounter fake news online.

chapters.title: Give system one a hard time
chapters.text:
From this summary so far, you could be forgiven for thinking that system one is nothing but a pesky troublemaker. However, that's not the case. Our brains need balance, and both systems allow us to make choices and form opinions. The problems only begin when we're unaware of the bias that can creep in and affect our decisions.

For instance, most of us look back at a successful event and don't think much about the steps taken to get there. We might assume that everything leading to that point was a success, too. However, there were likely to have been many speed bumps along the road.

Often, when we feel confident about something, we assume that we're right. We believe that our gut is guiding us. Yet, it just means that system one decided the information was pretty easy to handle and didn't need system two to help out. It jumps to conclusions and employs a "What You See Is All There Is" (WYSIATI) approach. So, we believe we're validated in our decision.

Thinking about the 'what if' isn't being overly cautious; it's key to making solid decisions.

The key to sound decision-making is using structured impressions and well-planned formulas. It means taking our time and thinking about all angles. While using our intuition is perfectly fine, a more focused approach is better in unfamiliar places and circumstances. Remember, intuition is primarily based on stored information, so we can't rely on it in situations we've never faced before. But what about optimism? Many people use this as a defense mechanism against doubt. It works sometimes, but being optimistic is the same as "hoping for the best." Instead, we should use the premortem tactic.

Here, we flip optimism on its head by assuming something has spectacularly failed. Rather than our minds tricking us, we turn the tables. Our brains kick into gear and try to work out what happened. We then use that information to identify potential hurdles and figure out how to fix them before they ever happen. 

Clever, right?

chapters.title: Emotional decisions are rarely the best
chapters.text:
We know that system one goes by what it has experienced, therefore creating memories, and system two works on logic, ruling our conscious thinking. The problem comes when trying to make a decision about something that we disliked in the past or felt emotional about. This situation causes a 'tug-of-war' effect between the two systems.

Otherwise, we run the risk of missing out on great adventures and opportunities.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Your experiences and biases shape your thoughts, and your thoughts affect your actions. So, you can see why it's so important to ensure that the choices you make are positive and not drenched in misinformation.

Ultimately, there’s nothing wrong with thinking fast, as long as you're sure that cognitive biases aren't at the heart of your decision. And, of course, sometimes thinking slow is just tiresome and energy-wasting.

Your brain is so complex that it's very easy to assume it's working for you in the right way. And most of the time, it is. But we live in a digital age where we get swamped with information at every turn. Taking the time to assess your thoughts and understand how they're formed is key to making solid choices based on what's best for you. After all, you wouldn't want to miss a fantastic opportunity, would you?

Try this

• For one week, before any significant decision, spend two minutes arguing for the opposite choice. This forces system two to highlight assumptions system one was hiding. You'll be amazed at what blind spots this simple exercise reveals.
• Try journaling for a few weeks and see if you can spot any patterns in your thoughts.
• Do these thoughts come from a place of truth, or are you being influenced by misinformation? Challenge any troublesome biases by reading solid facts and statistics.
• Every time you need to make a decision, stop for a second and think things through. Putting on the brakes will allow you to avoid unhelpful choices.
